reviews.review_text: Great for prototyping games. I had an idea for a tabletop game and could actually playtest it with friends without poorly printed props. Virtual table worked just fine!
you get to flick tokens at your friend who is kicking ur butt in the game. 10/10

As someone who is as poor as Ron Weasley but has the buying tastes of Paris Hilton, this game is an absolute godsend

I would prefer to have some of these board/card games as official DLC (like Unmatched or Arkham Horror) that way the creators still get the money for their hard work, but we can actually play with friends that can't travel or live far away. The controls look overwhelming but you only need to use a few of them tbh 

Overall though a fantastic way to play tabletop games! Highly recommend

I like this simulator but I do have slightly mixed feelings on it

First off, if you're playing on keyboard and mouse, it's about the most intuitive it could possibly get. The controls are nice(and rebindable if you don't like them), it's build primarily around this format.

The issues come in the form of other formats. It's very clearly not built for controller, which is fine but a little disappointing considering there is actually an option for controller input in the game itself, but I've yet to get it to work well or intuitively with my controller. It's a shame but it's fine. 
VR mode, which I was most interested in, is EGREGIOUSLY unintuitive, and that's even more of a shame because I can easily see this game being incredible in VR! There are so many strange decisions that have been made, such as binding movement to a single button that lets you swing around(instead of moving on an X/Y axis on a joystick), and the grab button not grabbing the object in the traditional way but instead sticking it to the end of your laser pointer and extending it to the end of the line as far out as it will go, the joysticks being an invisible selection wheel that has multiple keybinds but doesn't seem to do anything by default as far as I can tell? That sort of thing. The built in controls are terrible, and the way the customization of controls for VR are set up is even worse, because it's not set to things like "Flip" or "Search" or "Shuffle" in the bindings for the VR controllers, but instead are described as "North/East/South/West Click" and "Button 1-12"
I'm sure there's something I'm missing, and with enough research I could figure out how to make it more intuitive for me personally but I think it's more of the fact that it lacks the ease of use to begin with that bothers me.

One way or the other, it's a FANTASTIC tool for getting friends together to play card and board games, especially if you're playing it as intended. And who knows, Berserk is working on 2.0 right now so they might fix a lot of the complaints I have with it. We'll have to see! I complained a lot in this review but overall I'd say using it has been a positive experience and a new way to connect with my friends. Well done, 8/10
Just Fun with freinds and can play MTG and warhammer enough said

You like tabletop games? You enjoy reading rulebooks and then ignoring it, making up your own rules as you go? You don't have the option or time or whatever to sit down and play tabletop games IRL with people? Well here's the solution for you! Do all of these things but... ONLINE!! AT YOUR OWN PC!

+Tabletop games. Lots of em. Chess, Monopoly, Uno, Warhammer, Bolt Action, what was that mech one Battletech or something? Probably other stuff I've never heard of before. Don't like any of em? Make your own!
+Comes with all the "fun" stuff that a normal tabletop game comes with. Lots of dice, lots of cards, a rulebook people look over and ignore... The only thing you don't do is meet up IRL.

+/- Controls are a lil confusing at first but when you get used to it, they're good.

-Comes with all the "fun" stuff that a normal tabletop game comes with! Like kind of needing people to actually play. Setting the game up being really confusing at times. Tons of stuff to keep track of and sometimes some tools sometimes just your brain to help you keep track of them. I hope you like reading and rules and can remember these without having to re-read them every 5 minutes. And friends to play with, hopefully.

There's not a whole lot else to talk about. Sounds, music, graphics etc. don't really matter in this case and there's minimal sounds and basically no music anyways. Its just get together with a bunch of friends in a voice chat, open up a game, set it up, play. If you like tabletop games and have a community you can play with, no matter if its a big one or just a group of personal friends, then its a good game. If you mainly play singleplayer uh obviously thats not really gonna work. Wait for a discount *if* you have a community to play with. If you don't, skip this game entirely.
I can play the board games I own through this with people that can't come to my place every day to play them so I like it (no commas used).

This game is amazing. Community is thriving. I dont have enough Money, time, resources and space to get into certain hobbies. Or sometimes im looking into a new hobbie, card game, or war table top game to get into and this simulator is literally the best way to find out if im really interested or not. Its hard enough trying to convince a friend to put money into something they were never interested on. I play Union Arena TCG and not many people play this TCG.. but ofcourse TTS being the GOAT that it is had a simulator that is heavily supported by 3rd party websites that make it very user friendly. Convinced my friend who already had TTS to try out this mod and we ended up liking it so much we bought cards in real life! Im getting into another hobby called "Ember Obsidean Protocol" and of course someone out there decided people like me getting into these small niche games would like to try and play online with a friend.

🌟 Recommended
The ultimate sandbox for board game lovers, though it takes a moment to click.

Tabletop Simulator is hands down the best tool to play almost any board game with friends online, especially thanks to the massive Steam Workshop community. However, the user interface (UI) can feel quite complex and clunky during your first few sessions. Learning how to flip cards, lock objects, or manage the camera has a bit of a learning curve that might frustrate beginners.

The saving grace is how deeply customizable it is. Once you look past the initial complexity, the game offers tons of settings to tweak controls, physics, and shortcuts to your exact liking. As soon as you configure it to your preference, the gameplay becomes incredibly smooth. It requires a little patience at first, but it is absolutely worth it.

Tabletop sim has never been "good". Throughout the years I've used this game, I could only ever describe it as "enough"- as in, it does the job. It doesn't do it well, GOD does it not do it well. Crashes, desyncs, bad ui, horrendous player load times. But, right now, Its the only option on the market for what it does. When a proper, system agnostic alternative arrives, this game will die. And I won't mourn it.

but PLEASE make it 10$. I got it when it was on sale for 10$ because I REFUSE to pay any more than that for an online board game. 20$ is genuinely outrageous

[b]TABLETOP SIMULATOR[/b], is the PERFECT simulator for everything tabletop that you could think of. Board games, TTRPGs, jigsaw puzzles, whatever it is, you can do it here. Especially with the recent networking and engine upgrades there is little to complain about. Seriously. The majority of content is brought to you by the community through the workshop but there are some partnered games that have a paid DLC. With such an extensive catalogue, you will be extremely pressed to find a board game you won't be able to play on this. Even if there is, uploading, scanning and scripting is extremely easy through the creation tools. Most of the piece physics feel just right, and you are also free to alter them if you think dice are too floaty or cards don't place quite right. If you're reading this, and considering the purchase, just do it. It's like Wallpaper Engine, pay a low price and enjoy endless board games hand delivered to you. You can experience it in VR too if you want that experience

[b]PROS[/b]:
          - Customisable physics
          - Endless board games
          - Easy creation tools
          - VR

[b]CONS[/b]:
           - Can suffer from networking issues
